learn.title: India's National Green Hydrogen Mission
learn.intro: Green hydrogen is the 'Swiss Army Knife' of decarbonization, essential for cleaning up heavy industries like steel and refineries that cannot run on batteries alone. By using renewable power to split water, India aims to stop importing fossil fuels and become a global hub for clean energy production and export.
learn.c1_text: Electrolysis is like a chemical 'separator.' Using an electrolyzer, electricity splits water (H2O) into hydrogen and oxygen. While grey hydrogen uses gas, green hydrogen uses 100% renewable energy, ensuring the process emits zero carbon dioxide into the atmosphere.
learn.c2_text: The Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition (SIGHT) program provides financial incentives to lower the cost of electrolyzer manufacturing and hydrogen production, making clean fuel competitive with cheaper, polluting alternatives.
learn.c3_text: India targets 5 million metric tonnes of green hydrogen production per year by 2030. This shift will reduce fossil fuel imports and create thousands of green jobs across the industrial belt.
learn.stat1: Target: 5 MMT green hydrogen per year by 2030
learn.stat2: Target: 125 GW of renewable energy capacity added
learn.stat3: Reduction: Millions of tonnes of annual CO2 emissions
learn.stat4: Investment: Huge subsidies via SIGHT program

learn.title: Small Wind Turbines for Homes and Communities
learn.intro: While massive wind farms dominate the skyline, small-scale wind turbines empower individuals and remote communities to generate their own electricity. This decentralized approach reduces reliance on the main grid and provides a sustainable power source for farms, rural homes, and off-grid installations.
learn.c1_text: A wind turbine is like a reverse fan. Instead of using electricity to make wind, it uses wind to make electricity. The wind turns blades, which spin a shaft connected to a generator to produce power.
learn.c2_text: Placement is key; wind speed increases with height. Small turbines typically require a minimum average wind speed of 3-4 meters per second to be economically viable for a household.
learn.c3_text: In coastal states like Gujarat and Tamil Nadu, small wind turbines are ideal for augmenting solar pumps in agricultural settings, providing power during monsoon months when solar irradiation is low.
learn.stat1: Typical small turbine size: Under 100 kW
learn.stat3: Capacity factor: Varies by site location

learn.title: Smart Grids and Advanced Metering
learn.intro: Traditional power grids are one-way streets, but smart grids are a two-way conversation. By digitizing the system, utilities can balance supply and demand in real-time, integrating volatile renewable sources like solar and wind without risking blackouts or system instability.
learn.c1_text: Think of a smart grid as the internet for electricity. Instead of just sending power, the grid sends data, allowing the utility and the consumer to coordinate energy use based on availability.
learn.c2_text: Smart meters replace old dials. They record energy use in 15-minute intervals and transmit it wirelessly, enabling 'Time-of-Use' pricing where electricity is cheaper during off-peak hours.
learn.c3_text: India is implementing a massive smart metering program across DISCOMs to reduce Aggregate Technical and Commercial (AT&C) losses, which have historically been high in the Indian power sector.
learn.stat1: Reduction: Lower peak demand via demand response
learn.stat2: Efficiency: Real-time outage detection and recovery
learn.stat3: India: Millions of smart meters being deployed
learn.stat4: Impact: Significant reduction in billing errors

learn.title: Solar PV and the Photoelectric Effect
learn.intro: Solar Photovoltaics (PV) allow us to harvest the most abundant energy source in the universe: sunlight. By converting photons directly into electrons, PV panels provide a silent, emission-free way to power everything from a calculator to an entire city.
learn.c1_text: Imagine sunlight as a stream of tiny energy packets called photons. When these hit a silicon cell, they knock electrons loose, creating a flow of electricity—much like a billiard ball hitting another to start movement.
learn.c2_text: Solar cells use two layers of silicon (P-type and N-type) to create an internal electric field. This field acts like a one-way valve, forcing the loose electrons to flow in a specific direction.
learn.c3_text: India leverages its high solar insolation through massive parks like Bhadla Solar Park, utilizing PV technology to meet its ambitious target of 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030.

learn.title: How Electric Vehicles Work
learn.intro: Electric Vehicles (EVs) replace the internal combustion engine with a battery and a motor. This transition is critical because transport is one of the largest sources of urban air pollution and greenhouse gas emissions globally.
learn.c1_text: Instead of explosions in cylinders, EVs use magnetic fields. Electricity from the battery creates a rotating magnetic field that spins the motor, providing instant torque—like a light switch turning on a fan.
learn.c2_text: Regen braking is like a 'energy recycler.' When you lift your foot off the accelerator, the motor runs in reverse, acting as a generator that puts electricity back into the battery.
learn.c3_text: India's Faster Adoption and Manufacturing of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (FAME) policy provides subsidies to make EVs affordable, focusing heavily on 2-wheelers and 3-wheelers for urban mobility.
learn.stat1: Efficiency: EVs convert 77-82% of energy to power
learn.stat2: ICE Efficiency: Gas cars only convert ~12-30%
learn.stat3: India: Rapid growth in e-rickshaw adoption

learn.title: Geothermal Energy: Earth's Natural Heat
learn.intro: Geothermal energy taps into the immense heat stored beneath the Earth's crust. Unlike solar or wind, geothermal provides 'baseload' power, meaning it runs 24/7 regardless of weather, making it a reliable anchor for a clean energy grid.
learn.c1_text: Geothermal energy is like a giant natural radiator. We drill wells to bring hot water or steam from deep underground to the surface to spin turbines or heat buildings directly.
learn.c2_text: In binary plants, moderately hot water heats a 'working fluid' with a lower boiling point. This fluid vaporizes to spin the turbine, allowing power generation from cooler reservoirs.
learn.c3_text: India has significant potential in regions like Puga Valley (Ladakh) and Tattapani (Chhattisgarh), where the government is exploring geothermal heat for both electricity and direct heating applications.
learn.stat3: India: Potential sites identified in Ladakh
learn.stat4: Emission: Very low CO2 compared to coal
